questions.title: What surfaced during Phase 10 that triggered this insertion?
questions.context: Phase 10.1 was inserted into ROADMAP.md with no commit-trail context — the directory is empty (`.gitkeep` only). Goal text mentions 'accidental package sprawl' and manifest/boundary alignment, but recent commits are 10-02 dogfood work. This single answer narrows half the gray areas below; if you have a punch-list already (e.g. 'lmstudio-adapter has no no-fs gate; review depends on delivery; intent depends on authority'), drop it here verbatim.

options.label: Describe in chat_more
options.description: Open-ended — list the specific manifest/boundary issues you observed during Phase 10 that prompted carving 10.1 out.

questions.chat_more: The number of packages in the monorepo started to look hairy. This feels worth the time.

questions.title: Phase scope — manifests + enforcement only, or also source moves?
questions.context: Phase goal says 'package manifests and dependency-boundary enforcement.' Strict reading = package.json + contract tests + tsconfig refs only. Loose reading would include moving/merging/splitting packages (e.g. evaluation vs evaluation-runner, delivery vs delivery-runtime). The latter is a much bigger phase and should probably be its own thing.

options.label: Strict — manifests + enforcement only
options.description: package.json shape, dep declarations, no-fs/no-net contracts, tsconfig project references, AGENTS.md tier table updates. NO source moves, NO package merges/splits.

options.label: Strict + AGENTS.md prose
options.description: Above, plus rewrite AGENTS.md package-boundary section to reflect the new tier classifications and add explicit forbidden-edge documentation.

options.label: Loose — also restructure where boundaries are wrong
options.description: If the boundary problem is structural (e.g. review really does need to know about delivery), move/split/merge packages. Risk: phase blows up, dogfood loop on hold.

questions.title: Commit granularity
questions.context: 20 packages × manifest tweaks + ~6 new contract tests = ~30 atomic commits. Phase 1 used per-plan atomic commits in waves; Phase 10 has used larger feat/test pairs.

options.label: Per-package atomic plans
options.description: One PLAN per package being touched. Easy to revert, easy to review per-package, but plan count balloons.

options.label: Per-concern plans
options.description: Group by concern: 'tier classification + AGENTS table', 'no-fs contracts', 'tsconfig refs alignment', 'manifest hygiene sweep', 'back-edge fixes'. ~5–7 plans.

options.label: Single sweep plan
options.description: One plan that touches everything. Fast to land, hard to bisect, hard to review.

questions.title: Tier for `@protostar/lmstudio-adapter`
questions.context: Package depends on execution, intent, authority, repo, artifacts, review (heavy). Talks to LM Studio over HTTP — clearly network-permitted. Has NO `no-fs.contract.test.ts`. AGENTS.md tier table does NOT mention it. This is the most concrete boundary gap in the repo.

options.label: Domain network tier — add no-fs contract + AGENTS row
options.description: Treat identically to dogpile-adapter / delivery-runtime. Add `no-fs.contract.test.ts`. Add to AGENTS.md tier table under 'network-permitted, fs-forbidden'.

options.label: Domain network tier + audit dep list
options.description: Above, plus audit the heavy dep list (intent, authority, repo, artifacts, review, execution) — `repo` in particular is suspect for a fs-forbidden package since `repo` IS the fs tier. Decide if any deps should be peerDeps or dropped.

options.label: Promote `lmstudio-adapter` to fs-permitted
options.description: If LM Studio adapter genuinely needs fs (e.g. to stream model output to disk), bless it. Risk: another package in the fs tier weakens the tier guarantee.

questions.title: Tier for `@protostar/evaluation-runner`
questions.context: Already has `no-fs.contract.test.ts`. Depends on dogpile-adapter, dogpile-types, evaluation, intent, planning, review. Not in AGENTS.md tier table.

options.label: Pure tier — add no-net contract too
options.description: If it's pure (no fs proven by existing contract), prove no-net too. Add a `no-net.contract.test.ts` scanning for fetch/http imports. Add to AGENTS row under 'pure'.

options.label: Domain network tier — runs piles via dogpile-adapter
options.description: It coordinates judges via dogpile-adapter (which IS network-permitted), so transitively it's a network-using package. Add to AGENTS row under 'network-permitted, fs-forbidden'.

options.label: Pure — leave as-is, just document
options.description: It's pure-orchestration: it composes pile presets but the network call lives in dogpile-adapter. Add an AGENTS row under 'pure' with a note that network exits via dogpile-adapter.

questions.title: Tiers for `mechanical-checks`, `repair`, `fixtures`, `admission-e2e`
questions.context: None are in the AGENTS.md tier table. mechanical-checks depends on execution/repo/intent/review (heavy, but presumably pure). repair depends on planning/intent. fixtures has no deps. admission-e2e is test-only (devDep'd everywhere; aggregates contract tests).

options.label: All four = pure tier; admission-e2e = test-only sub-tier
options.description: Add four AGENTS rows. Introduce a 'test-only' sub-tier explicitly so admission-e2e's coupling-to-everything is sanctioned, not accidental. test-only packages can depend on any tier but nothing depends on them.

options.label: Skip the test-only distinction
options.description: List admission-e2e under 'pure' with a note. Less ceremony, but blurs the conceptual line between 'production code that's pure' and 'aggregator of contract tests'.

options.label: Don't classify in AGENTS — only add no-fs/no-net contracts where missing
options.description: Keep AGENTS table minimal (only domain-network and fs tiers). Add contracts to the pure ones. Risk: tier table stays incomplete and a future package slips through unclassified.

questions.title: Declare tier in `package.json` (custom field)?
questions.context: Currently tier is documented only in AGENTS.md prose. A `protostar.tier` field in each package.json would make tier machine-readable and drive a single workspace-wide conformance test (Q-11).

options.label: Yes — add `protostar.tier: 'pure' | 'fs' | 'network' | 'orchestration' | 'test-only'`
options.description: Single source of truth; conformance test reads manifests and validates contract presence + dep direction. Manifest hygiene + enforcement co-located.

options.label: No — keep tier in AGENTS.md prose, derive from contract presence
options.description: A package's tier is implied by which contract tests it ships. Less ceremony, but tier is invisible to humans reading package.json.

options.label: Yes, but in a single workspace-root file
options.description: `.planning/package-tiers.json` or `tools/tiers.json` lists every package and its tier. One file to edit when adding a package, no per-package.json drift.

questions.title: `@protostar/intent` → `@protostar/authority` back-edge
questions.context: intent's package.json declares `@protostar/authority` as a dep. Conceptually intent is the front door (admission), authority is the governance kernel that runs LATER. authority already depends on intent. This forms a cycle in concept, even if TypeScript accepts it.

options.label: Flip — move whatever intent uses from authority into intent (or a shared types pkg)
options.description: Identify the symbol(s) intent imports from authority, move them upstream. Likely a shared 'authority-types' that both can depend on, or just lift into intent.

options.label: Accept — authority types are needed at intent-confirmation time
options.description: If `ConfirmedIntent` carries an authority envelope, intent legitimately needs authority's type definitions. Document the edge as deliberate in AGENTS.md.

options.label: Investigate first — add a Q for the planner
options.description: Defer the decision: the planner's first task is to grep the actual import and decide a/b based on what's imported.

questions.title: `@protostar/review` → `@protostar/delivery` and `@protostar/repair` back-edges
questions.context: review depends on delivery, dogpile-adapter, dogpile-types, execution, repair, repo. Review is the central control loop, but review depending on delivery (which is downstream) and repair (which review TRIGGERS) is suspect. If review imports delivery only for refusal-shape types, that's a smell pointing at a missing shared types package.

options.label: Extract shared refusal/verdict types into a thin types package
options.description: Create `@protostar/review-types` (or similar) that both review and delivery depend on. Eliminates the back-edge cleanly.

options.label: Flip — delivery and repair depend on review, not vice versa
options.description: Move whatever review imports from delivery/repair into review itself. delivery's PR-readiness check would import review's verdict type, not the reverse.

options.label: Accept and document — review is the orchestration spine
options.description: If review legitimately needs to know delivery+repair shapes to drive the loop, document it in AGENTS.md as 'review is the orchestration package; back-edges expected.'

options.label: Defer to planner — grep imports first
options.description: Same as Q-08(c): planner inspects actual imports and picks a/b/c per back-edge.

questions.title: `@protostar/delivery-runtime` → `@protostar/review` back-edge
questions.context: delivery-runtime depends on review (probably for `ReviewVerdict` to gate PR opening). delivery-runtime is the network-tier executor; review is upstream. Same shape as Q-09.

options.label: Same fix as Q-09
options.description: If Q-09 extracts a types package, this back-edge collapses too. Treat as same decision.

options.label: Accept — delivery-runtime gates on review verdict, must import its type
options.description: PR delivery is conditional on review pass; importing the verdict type is the right shape.

options.label: Flip — review imports a delivery-readiness predicate
options.description: Review knows whether a result is 'pr-ready'; that predicate lives in review, not in delivery-runtime checking review's verdict.

questions.title: Workspace-wide tier-conformance test vs per-package contracts
questions.context: Existing pattern: each network-tier package ships its own `no-fs.contract.test.ts`. Phase 10 Q-21 puts a single `authority-boundary.contract.test.ts` in admission-e2e. Two coexisting patterns; 10.1 should pick one.

options.label: Per-package contracts (current pattern, expanded)
options.description: Every pure package ships `no-fs.contract.test.ts` + `no-net.contract.test.ts`. Every network-tier ships `no-fs.contract.test.ts`. Tests live next to source. Pattern is already established for 4 packages.

options.label: Single workspace-wide conformance test in admission-e2e
options.description: One test reads `pnpm-workspace.yaml` (or the tier manifest from Q-07), iterates packages, asserts each one's tier rules. Same shape as Phase 10's authority-boundary contract. One file to maintain.

options.label: Both — workspace-wide is the gate, per-package contracts stay as defense-in-depth
options.description: Workspace-wide test is authoritative; per-package contracts catch local edits without running the e2e suite. More files but tighter feedback loop.

questions.title: Dependency-direction enforcement tool
questions.context: Today: ad-hoc. Options: (a) write a custom workspace-wide test that reads each package.json's `@protostar/*` deps and asserts a layered DAG, (b) adopt `dependency-cruiser` (mature, configurable), (c) `eslint-plugin-import/no-restricted-paths`, (d) skip — rely on code review.

options.label: Custom workspace-wide test (matches Q-11b shape)
options.description: Read manifests, build dep graph, assert no cycles + assert tier-respecting edges. Pure TS, no new dev-dep, matches existing contract-test pattern.

options.label: `dependency-cruiser`
options.description: Industry-standard, rich rule DSL, JSON config. New dev-dep (mind the runtime-deps lock posture; this would be dev-only). Best ergonomics; weakest 'we own this' story.

options.label: Skip — rely on Q-11 contracts + review
options.description: no-fs/no-net contracts are the load-bearing invariant; full DAG enforcement might be overkill for 20 packages.

questions.title: tsconfig project-references alignment with package.json deps
questions.context: When a package.json adds/drops a `@protostar/*` dep, its `tsconfig.json` `references` array must match or `tsc -b` silently uses stale dist. Easy to drift. A simple workspace test could assert `package.json @protostar/* deps == tsconfig references`.

options.label: Add a workspace test asserting tsconfig refs == package.json deps
options.description: Roll into the same workspace-wide conformance test from Q-11/Q-12. Pure read of two files per package.

options.label: Defer — only fix observed drift
options.description: Audit current state, fix what's drifted, but don't add an enforcement test. Risk: drifts again silently.

options.label: Skip — `tsc -b` failure modes are visible enough
options.description: Modern pnpm + tsc -b surfaces missing refs as build errors fast. Don't add tooling for an invariant the compiler enforces.

questions.title: `exports` field shape across packages
questions.context: Phase 10 Q-17 locks all packages public. That means npm consumers see `exports`. Some packages declare subpath exports (`./schema`, `./artifacts`); some only have `.`. Public-package consumers benefit from a uniform shape.

options.label: Uniform — every package exports `.` + `./package.json`, subpaths only when explicitly justified
options.description: Minimal surface; subpath exports are explicit feature decisions. Reduces accidental coupling on internal modules.

options.label: Uniform — every package documents allowed subpaths in its README; knip enforces
options.description: Phase 10 Q-19 already runs knip; extend to enforce 'no import from a non-exported subpath.' Tied to per-package READMEs from Phase 10 Q-13.

options.label: Audit current state, no uniform rule
options.description: Document what each package exports, fix obvious mistakes, don't enforce a shape.

questions.title: `sideEffects: false` declarations
questions.context: Public ESM packages benefit from `"sideEffects": false` for tree-shaking by downstream bundlers. Currently inconsistent across packages.

options.label: Add `sideEffects: false` to every pure package; omit for fs/network tiers
options.description: Pure packages are by definition side-effect-free at import time. Tier table from Q-04..Q-07 makes this mechanical.

options.label: Skip — node consumers don't tree-shake; we have no browser surface
options.description: Tauri toy doesn't import @protostar/* packages; node consumers don't benefit. Don't add fields with no current consumer.

questions.title: `engines` and `packageManager` field consistency
questions.context: Project locks Node >=22 (PROJECT.md) and pnpm via root package.json. Per-package `engines.node` declarations would enforce at install time for npm consumers; currently inconsistent.

options.label: Add `engines.node: '>=22'` to every published package; root keeps `packageManager`
options.description: Makes the Node-22 floor visible to npm consumers; consistent with public-package posture.

options.label: Root only — packages inherit
options.description: Less drift surface; downside is npm consumers don't see the Node floor in the package they install.

questions.title: `peerDependencies` for cross-package types
questions.context: Currently `@protostar/*` cross-deps are all hard `dependencies: workspace:*`. For published packages, this means installing one package pulls every transitive @protostar dep. Some shared-type packages might be more naturally `peerDependencies`.

options.label: Keep all cross-deps as `dependencies`
options.description: Simplest; npm consumers get a working install. Cost: heavier install footprint.

options.label: Make pure types-only packages `peerDependencies`
options.description: If `@protostar/intent` is consumed primarily for its types, peerDep avoids version-skew issues. More install ceremony for consumers.

options.label: Defer — revisit when first external consumer reports a problem
options.description: Speculative optimization; v0.1 has no external consumers yet. Lock simpler shape now, optimize later.

questions.title: `apps/factory-cli` published as `@protostar/factory-cli`?
questions.context: Phase 10 Q-17 note says 'apps/factory-cli publishes as @protostar/factory-cli with a bin entry.' But `apps/*` is conventionally non-publishable in monorepos. Decide whether 10.1 actually wires this and confirms the bin entry shape.

options.label: Yes — confirm and wire per Phase 10 Q-17 note
options.description: Add `publishConfig`, `bin`, validate the binary works post-install. CLI becomes `npx @protostar/factory-cli` for external users.

options.label: Defer — CLI publish is an operator-surface decision belonging to Phase 11+
options.description: Phase 11 is headless mode; CLI distribution may change shape. Don't lock the bin contract here.

options.label: No — apps/* stay private; only packages/* publish
options.description: Reverses the Phase 10 Q-17 note. Only library consumers get @protostar/* packages; CLI distribution is via repo clone for v0.1.
